author: @GarthClarkson
text:
Great video. Love your work. I live off grid and you have given me much inspiration and saved me from a harsh winter here in the mountains. 
Just a short tip that might help you with sheet metal work. Instead of bashing the sheet with a hammer or old axle shaft over a bit of 4" pipe, just use a small to medium (8" - 200mm) shifting spanner. Adjust the spanner gap to just a little wider than the thickness of the sheet metal and slide it over the edge to the depth of where you want the bend, then you just bend the metal with the spanner to the desired angle then move it the width of the jaws sideways and repeat until it has covered the edge that you wanted to bend. 
Repeat the process until the desired angle is achieved.
You can then finish it off with the hammer. 
This should save you LOTS of time and can be very precise.

author: @Berkana
text: Robert, it isn't clear from this video whether the part that comes in contact with fire is made of teflon coated non-stick material. That material must not be exposed to temperatures above roughly 230˚C because it breaks down into toxic perfluoro-chemicals ("forever chemicals") which not only have no natural pathway to decompose, but are endocrine disruptors and carcinogens. It is not good to breathe the vapors of such chemicals. My concern with taking things like cake and pie tins to make a stove like this is that the stove seems to me to likely heat to temperatures above what is safe for non-stick coatings.

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text:
The bitter truth is that enthusiasm for the subject will not feed you and will not provide you with shelter.
I have no idea how teacher's wage looks like in the UK, but in Poland it is miserable.
I was studying physics (just because i love to know how things work) and by some chance i ended up on teacher's speciality. I can tell you that out of 130 students beginning this field of study, maybe 10 was really fit to be amazing teachers.
As for me i ended up working as a service tech. In the beginning it was resident maintenance on the production line of cigarettes. Now i specialize in high bay warehouse automation as visiting maintenance for a bigger company. Way more money, although sometimes because of dangers it sometimes also doesn't feel enough 😁
But i really respect people with such enthusiasm and teaching skills (my personal favourite two: Feynman and Niels deGrasse Tyson). I had quite a few teachers in my family so this kind of skill is close to my heart and whenever i can i try to play a teacher role to any kid willing to listen a bit about physics 😉

author: @docink6175
text: I use smaller versions of this a lot, it needs some way to regulate the amount of flame, I just use the top of the can Im using as the fuel container which can also be used to put it out. My favorite way to prevent sloshing is sand but wool, cotton. toilet paper etc will hold the fuel better if the cooker/heater is knocked over.. this was another really great build! Im looking forward to the conversion

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: on it now mate!

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: Gas is a fuel. Alcohol is a fuel. Wood is a fuel...  I wouldn't use any petro-chemicals, but plenty of fuel is perfectly fine and in some cases even adds to the experience (appropriate woods and charcoal etc)

replies.author: @emeryz10
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering, I’ve recently bought two of them on offer up for only $120. I wanted to see if I could utilize the energy being released from the stack indoors without the risk of back pressure while still allowing it to vent to atmosphere.

author: @D-B-Cooper
text: Non stick coating is poisonous when burned. For a little more money you could have gotten stainless. A lot of the cost of a marine stove is the gimbal and sea rail.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: these tins don't have teflon on them they are stove painted - you can buy them in just plain steel if you want - most marine stoves don't have gimbals - it so they can sell them to the camping folks too - the gimbal is often the cabinet work the stove fits into - actually it's little more than a swinging tray metal lined - I don't see where the money is tbh

replies.author: @D-B-Cooper
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering you are obviously not a boat person. A proper boat stove is made of materials suitable for a salt environment, with weighted gimble and has safety features to be used in a contained area. Also has a way of keeping the pots from being thrown off.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @D-B-Cooper lolol - I see - ok mate whatever lol

author: @gedreillyhomestead6926
text: I was waiting to see how you gimbaled it! I always thought that marine stoves were on 2 way gimbals. 🤔 Ah well, you lives and learns. 👍

replies.author: @dantronics1682
replies.text: could be for river boats or barge

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: No gimbal necessary. That's what the rock wool is for.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: most marine stoves don't have gimbals - it so they can sell them to the camping folks too - the gimbal is often the cabinet work the stove fits into - actually it's little more than a swinging tray metal lined - I have never seen a double set of gimbals

author: @smithsmarine4885
text: the reason they use meths in the origo marine stoves (What this is a copy of) is its hydroscopic (it attracts water) what do you have in abundance on a boat WATER. so in the event of it getting out of hand an ever present extinguisher is always there. much safer than gas (propane).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - and yes it is basically a copy of the Origo - a very nice stove

author: @enkhyy
text: Put it in a gimbal mount and you are set for a cruise.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: most marine stoves don't have gimbals - it so they can sell them to the camping folks too - the gimbal is often the cabinet work the stove fits into - actually it's little more than a swinging tray metal lined.

replies.author: @enkhyy
replies.text: Older sailboats were more likely to have this feature. Newer boats may have more creature comforts and customization.
